name: What to Do When Your Paycheck Stops After a Work Injury: Navigating Temporary Disability Benefits in Utah and Idaho
description: What to Do When Your Paycheck Stops After a Work Injury: Navigating Temporary Disability Benefits in Utah and Idaho - Salt Lake City

step.name: Step 1
step.text: Getting injured on the job is traumatic enough, but the stress often doubles when you realize you are physically unable to return to work and your regular paycheck has ceased. In Utah and Idaho, workers’ compensation is designed to provide a safety net known as Temporary Disability (TD) benefits. However, securing these benefits isn't always automatic, and the process can be fraught with bureaucratic hurdles.

step.name: Step 2
step.text: ### What to Do First

step.name: Step 3
step.text: The moments and days immediately following a workplace injury are critical. To ensure you qualify for Temporary Disability benefits, you must take these steps:

step.name: Step 4
step.text: 1. **Report the Injury Immediately:** Notify your supervisor or HR department in writing. In Utah, you generally have 180 days to report, but doing it immediately prevents the insurance company from claiming the injury happened elsewhere. In Idaho, the window is even tighter.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: 2. **Seek Medical Attention:** Visit an authorized medical provider. You must explicitly tell the doctor that your injury happened at work.

step.name: Step 6
step.text: 3. **Obtain a Work Status Note:** This is the most important document for TD benefits. Your doctor must provide a written statement stating either that you are "off work" entirely or that you have "work restrictions" (such as no lifting over 5 pounds) that your employer cannot accommodate.

step.name: Step 7
step.text: ### How the Temporary Disability Process Works

step.name: Step 8
step.text: Temporary Disability benefits are intended to replace a portion of your lost wages while you are recovering. There are generally two types:

step.name: Step 9
step.text: *   **Temporary Total Disability (TTD):** Paid when your doctor says you cannot work at all during your recovery.

step.name: Step 10
step.text: *   **Temporary Partial Disability (TPD):** Paid when you can work light duty, but you are earning less than your pre-injury wages.

step.name: Step 11
step.text: In both Utah and Idaho, these benefits typically cover approximately two-thirds (66.67%) of your average weekly wage, subject to state maximums. Benefits do not start the very first day you miss work; there is usually a "waiting period" (3 days in Utah, 5 days in Idaho). If you remain off work for more than 14 days, you are typically reimbursed for that initial waiting period.

step.name: Step 12
step.text: ### Key Evidence and Documents

step.name: Step 13
step.text: To keep your benefits flowing without interruption, you must maintain a paper trail:

step.name: Step 14
step.text: *   **Medical Progress Reports:** The insurance company requires proof that you are still receiving treatment and are still unable to work.

step.name: Step 15
step.text: *   **Wage Records:** Provide your attorney or the insurance adjuster with pay stubs from the 26 to 52 weeks prior to your injury to ensure your benefit rate is calculated correctly.

step.name: Step 16
step.text: *   **Written Contact Logs:** Keep a record of every time your employer says they "don't have light duty available." This is proof that you are entitled to TTD even if you have partial restrictions.

step.name: Step 17
step.text: ### Common Mistakes to Avoid

step.name: Step 18
step.text: Many workers inadvertently sabotage their own benefits by:

step.name: Step 19
step.text: *   **Missing Appointments:** If you miss a doctor’s appointment, the insurance company may argue you have recovered or are non-compliant, giving them a reason to stop payments.

step.name: Step 20
step.text: *   **Working "Under the Table":** If you are caught performing any work while receiving TD benefits, you could face fraud charges and lose all rights to future compensation.

step.name: Step 21
step.text: *   **Exceeding Restrictions at Home:** Insurance investigators often film injured workers doing yard work or grocery shopping. If your doctor said "no lifting," and you are caught lifting a bag of mulch, your benefits will be terminated.

step.name: Step 22
step.text: ### When to Contact a Lawyer

step.name: Step 23
step.text: Temporary disability disputes often arise when the insurance company’s doctor (the IME doctor) claims you are ready to return to work, but your own treating physician disagrees. This "conflict of medical evidence" is a primary reason to seek legal counsel. Attorneys like Halston T. Davis or Mark J. Sanchez can help file an adjudication or request a hearing to restore your benefits.

step.name: Step 24
step.text: ### Next Steps for the Injured Worker

step.name: Step 25
step.text: If your benefits have been delayed or denied, start by requesting a copy of your "Claim Status Report" from the insurance carrier. Reach out to an experienced workers' compensation attorney to review your average weekly wage calculation—errors here can cost you thousands of dollars over the life of a claim. At Just Work Comp Law, we offer free consultations to help you determine if you are receiving the full amount allowed under Utah or Idaho law.
